title: Chapter 30: The Road Downtown
content: The SUV ate the ruined highway like it was hungry for it.

Marcus drove with one hand on the wheel and the other on the system-inventory interface, scrolling through the newly unlocked Expedition category. Most of the entries were locked—greyed-out icons with level requirements he hadn't hit yet—but the ones he could see made his pulse quicken.

**[EXPEDITION SUPPLY PACK — Available]**
**[MOBILE COMMAND BLUEPRINT — Available]**
**[TERRAIN SCANNER — Level 15 Required]**

"Keep your eyes on the road," Elena said from the back seat, her voice carrying the particular exhaustion of someone who'd been packing medical supplies for four hours straight. "The system will still be there when we're not wrapped around a lamppost."
"Statistically unlikely," Marcus said. "There are no more lampposts. Just lamppost corpses."

He wasn't exaggerating. The suburbs had been brutalized. Every hundred yards brought another tableau of collapse—a minivan wrapped around a fire hydrant, its windows starred with blood but no bodies inside. A school bus on its side, luggage spilled across three lanes like someone had turned a suitcase inside out. A house with its garage door hanging open, the family SUV still inside, keys in the ignition, the driver's seat stained dark where someone had been pulled out through the window.

Sera crouched on the roof, one hand braced on the luggage rack, her white hair streaming behind her like a war banner. She'd insisted on the position—"I can see further and kill faster from up there"—and Marcus hadn't argued. She was right. She was always right about violence.

The counter-frequency emitter mounted on the roof hummed at the edge of his hearing, pushing back the zombie proximity warnings like a force field. Within fifty meters of the SUV, the scattered dead turned away, their moth-to-flame instinct overridden by something they found intolerable about the sound.
Elena leaned forward between the front seats. "How far to the downtown perimeter?"
"Eight miles. Maybe six as the crow flies." Marcus checked the rearview mirror. "Of course, crows don't have to navigate through—" He stopped.
Sera's hand slapped the roof twice. The signal for *stop*.
Marcus braked. The SUV growled to a halt in the middle of the highway, surrounded by the skeletal frames of abandoned cars.

Through the windshield, a hundred yards ahead: a military checkpoint. Sandbags. Jersey barriers. A .50 cal mount, the gun still pointing outward. And behind the barriers, the dark stain of old fire—someone had made a stand here and lost.
But that wasn't what made Sera signal the stop.
Voices. Faint. Carried on the morning wind from somewhere to the east.
Walkie-talkie chatter.
Marcus killed the engine. The silence that followed was immediate and total—just the tick of cooling metal and his own heartbeat.
"Raiders," Sera said through the open sunroof. Her voice was flat. Evaluating. "Four of them. Two hundred meters, northeast, behind that convoy."

Marcus followed her gaze. A flipped transport truck had created a natural barricade, and behind it, he could just make out movement—figures in tactical gear, armed, moving with the casual confidence of people who owned the territory they walked through.
"Crane's people?" Elena whispered.
"Gotta be." Marcus's mouth was dry. "Vivienne said he controls downtown. These are his patrols."
Sera dropped from the roof with the soundless grace of a cat. She landed in a crouch beside the driver's door, her grey eyes already tracking the patrol's movement pattern.
"Four men. Two carry rifles, one has a shotgun, one has a sidearm and a radio." Her voice was clinical. "The radio operator has checked in twice in the last ninety seconds. They're on a schedule."
Marcus looked at her. "If they check in and don't get an answer—"
"Then Crane knows someone is in his territory. Yes." No hesitation. "I can eliminate all four before the next check-in. The interval is forty-five seconds. I need eleven."
He stared at her. "Eleven seconds?"
"Twelve if one of them runs." She tilted her head. "Eight if you don't care about noise."
"Go."
She was already gone—a blur of white and shadow that vanished between the cars and didn't come back.
Marcus pressed his back against the seat and counted. One. Two. Three.
The walkie-talkie on the nearest raider's hip crackled. He reached for it.
Seven. Eight.
Something moved behind the convoy. A soft sound—wet, brief, final.
Ten.

The radio operator turned, his hand still reaching for the walkie-talkie, and Sera was already there. She took him apart with the efficiency of someone disassembling a clock. No sound. No struggle. Just a body folding in on itself and not getting up.
Eleven.
The highway was quiet again.
Twelve. Thirteen.
Nothing moved.
"Sera?" Marcus said quietly.

"Clear." Her voice came from behind the convoy, slightly breathless. Not from exertion—from restraint. She'd had to be fast instead of slow, surgical instead of devastating. Speed took more out of her than violence did.
He got out of the SUV. Elena stayed inside, hand on her medical kit, watching the windows.
The four raiders lay in a rough line behind the transport truck. Clean work—necks and spines, no mess. Sera stood over them, her expression unreadable.
"Search them," she said. "Quickly. The next patrol comes through in twenty minutes."

Marcus knelt beside the radio operator. The walkie-talkie was still clipped to the dead man's belt, still crackling with intervals of static. He unclipped it and thumbed through the channels until he found the active one.
"—Delta-three, report. Delta-three, you're past check-in. Report or we send a team."
He muted it. That bought them maybe ten minutes before Crane's people investigated.
The raider's vest had a pocket. Inside: a laminated card, partially smeared with blood but still readable.

**CRANE TERRITORIES — PATROL MANIFEST**
**Zone 4: Northwestern Perimeter**
**Priority Target: "New Eden" — Suburban Compound**
**Asset Assessment: Food stores (confirmed), Power source (confirmed), System Host (unconfirmed)**
**Recon-in-Force: 14-day timeline pending authorization**
Marcus read it twice. Then a third time, because the words didn't want to make sense.
"Marcus." Sera's voice was sharp. "What is it?"
He held up the card. She took it, her grey eyes scanning the text, and for a moment the Zombie Queen's composure cracked—just a fissure, a hairline fracture of genuine alarm.
"System Host," she read aloud. "He knows."
"Not exactly." Marcus took the card back. "He suspects. 'Unconfirmed.' But he knows about New Eden, he knows we have food and power, and he's planning to come take it."
Elena leaned out the window. "How long do we have?"
"Fourteen days. If this is current." He folded the card and shoved it into his pocket. "But I just killed his patrol. When they don't report back, that timeline might accelerate."
Sera looked toward the downtown skyline—towers of glass and steel rising from the urban corpse like tombstones.
"Then we move faster," she said. "Lily doesn't have fourteen days. And neither does New Eden."
She was back on the roof before Marcus could respond, a pale sentinel against the grey sky, her eyes fixed on the towers where her sister waited in the dark.
Marcus started the engine. The SUV rolled forward, past the dead patrol, past the military checkpoint, past the last outpost of the world that used to exist.
Downtown waited.

**[EXPEDITION UPDATE: Hostile Territory Entered]**
**[Crane's Raiders: AWARE of New Eden]**
**[Lily Vale: Signal weakening. 36 hours remaining.]**
**[Recommended: Increase pace. No further stops.]**
